Lab 3: Baseline Model — Simple Linear Regression

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

CRISP-DM Phases 3 & 4 — Data Preparation & Modelling

Author

Prof. Dr. Jörg Schoder

Published

today

1 Learning Objectives and Overview

NoteLearning Objectives
  • Understand simple linear regression as a descriptive tool: conditional means, not causal claims
  • Read and interpret the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression equation and its coefficients correctly
  • Compute and interpret the coefficient of determination R² — and understand why a low R² is information, not failure
  • Use residuals as a measure of store-level underperformance
  • Diagnose Omitted Variable Bias (OVB) visually in the residual plot

Scenario: In Lab 2 you explored the Carseats data and established that Price is negatively correlated with Sales (\(r \approx -0.44\)). Now you build the simplest possible regression model — the baseline — and critically assess what it reveals and what it hides.

2 🧭 How to Navigate This Lab

Symbol Box colour Meaning When to read
⚠️ Red — Action Required Run code, interpret output, answer questions Do this in the live session
💡 Green — Best Practice / Tip Coding tips, R functions, shortcuts Skim in the lab
📚 Blue, collapsed — Background Theory Concepts, theory, derivations Read at home
ℹ️ Blue, open — Note Short clarifications Read when reached

⏱ 40 minutes for mandatory tasks (A1–A4). Bonus tasks if time permits.

3 Icebreaker: The Same Line — Different Worlds

In Lab 2 the Datasaurus Dozen showed that identical summary statistics can hide completely different data structures. Regression is no different: four datasets can yield the same fitted line — identical slope, intercept, and \(R^2\). Fitting a line and reading its equation is not enough.

Show code: Anscombe summary statistics 
# Anscombe's Quartet: four datasets with (almost) identical regression statistics
anscombe_tidy <- anscombe %>%
  mutate(observation = seq_len(n())) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(-observation,
                      names_to = c(".value", "set"),
                      names_pattern = "(.)(.)")  %>%
  mutate(set = c("I", "II", "III", "IV")[as.integer(set)])

# Summary: same correlation, same slope, same R² across all four
anscombe_tidy %>%
  group_by(set) %>%
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    mean_x   = round(mean(x), 2),
    mean_y   = round(mean(y), 2),
    cor_xy   = round(cor(x, y), 3),       # Pearson correlation
    slope    = round(coef(lm(y ~ x))[2], 3),  # OLS slope
    r_sq     = round(cor(x, y)^2, 3)      # R² = r² (only for simple regression)
  ) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "Anscombe's Quartet --- summary statistics",
    subtitle = "All four datasets have nearly identical regression statistics"
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = "13px")
Anscombe's Quartet --- summary statistics
All four datasets have nearly identical regression statistics
set n mean_x mean_y cor_xy slope r_sq
I 11 9 7.5 0.816 0.5 0.667
II 11 9 7.5 0.816 0.5 0.666
III 11 9 7.5 0.816 0.5 0.666
IV 11 9 7.5 0.817 0.5 0.667

Now look at the diagram showing the data and the fitted OLS line:

Show code: Anscombe scatter plots 
anscombe_tidy %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(color = "#7d0a52", size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8) +   # raw observations
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE,                      # OLS line
              color = "#D50006", linewidth = 1.2) +
  facet_wrap(~set, ncol = 4) +                                # one panel per dataset
  # --- labels ---
  labs(
    title    = "Anscombe's Quartet --- same line, different worlds",
    subtitle = "Panel I: linear ✓ | II: curved | III: outlier in y | IV: single high-leverage outlier",
    x = "x", y = "y"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))
ImportantThe Central Lesson for Regression

Panel I shows a genuinely linear relationship: here the OLS line is appropriate. Panels II–IV show cases where the same line misleads: curvature, a single outlier, and a single high-leverage outlier each produce the same fitted line but completely different underlying structures.

Consequence: Fitting a regression model is not enough. The residuals must always be inspected visually. This is what Step 3 of this lab teaches.

In this lab (as in the whole session 1) we treat our data as being the complete population (descriptive regression). Then the predicted values \(\hat{y}_i\) on the OLS line are interpreted as the conditional means: what a store on average achieves at a given price. The residual \(e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\) measures the deviation from this benchmark. Note that the terminology of the -output in the regression table is potentially misleading since the column “estimate” has an inferential connotation.

3.1 Background: Where We Are in CRISP-DM

In Lab 2 (Phase 2: Data Understanding) we established the key EDA findings: Price is negatively correlated with Sales, ShelveLoc creates large group differences, and the log transformation makes Sales distribution worse (more skewed), not better. Now we enter Phases 3 & 4: we prepare the data and fit the first model.

Phase Name Content Status
1 Business Understanding Goal: identify underperforming stores Done (lecture)
2 Data Understanding EDA: distributions, correlations, OVB signals Done (Lab 2)
3 Data Preparation Factor recoding, zero handling, variable selection Done (Lab 2)
4 Modelling Baseline model: Sales ~ Price ← This lab
5 Evaluation R², residuals, OVB diagnosis ← This lab
6 Deployment Performance ranking, managerial memo Lab 4

3.2 Background: Simple Linear Regression as a Descriptive Tool

3.2.1 What Regression Is — and What It Is Not

The term regression analysis is typically associated with inferential statistics: estimating parameters from a sample and making claims about a larger population. But regression is a broader concept, that can also be used as a descriptive technique for summarising relationships in data. This lab covers only the descriptive case. That means we treat our data on the 400 stores as complete population. Hence we do not need to generalize from a sample to a larger world. We are describing what is already in front of us.

Note📚 Background: The Etymology and History of Regression

The term regression derives from the Latin regredi (to go back, to return). It was introduced into statistics by Francis Galton (1822–1911), who observed that the children of unusually tall parents tend to be taller than average — but not as tall as their parents. Extreme characteristics regress towards the mean across generations.

The modern statistical meaning is broader: regression is any method that describes how the conditional mean of one variable changes with another. The “returning” interpretation is now purely etymological.

When we want to summarize how the average sales change with price across the 400 stores, the simplest way to do this is to fit a straight line through the scatter plot of Sales (dependent variable) vs. Price (independent variable). Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) fits this line by finding the line that minimizes the sum of squared vertical distances from the data points to the line. In this sense the OLS line is the best description of how average sales vary with price across the 400 stores we have.

Note📚 Background: Exact vs. Statistical Relationships

In some situations, the relationship between two variables is exact and deterministic. At a petrol station, the bill is exactly proportional to the number of litres filled: \(y = p \cdot x\), with no scatter around the line.

In most business situations, the relationship is statistical: the same price does not always produce the same sales, because other factors — shelf location, advertising, local competition, demographics — also play a role. The OLS line captures the systematic part of this relationship; the residuals capture the rest.

\[y_i = \underbrace{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i}_{\text{systematic}} + \underbrace{e_i}_{\text{unsystematic}}\]

The statistical relationship is not a defect — it is the reality of business data. The residuals are the starting point for understanding why some stores deviate from the systematic pattern (Stoetzer (2020, 47)).

3.3 Regression as Conditional Means

In descriptive statistics, the OLS line is a machine for computing conditional means:

\[\hat{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot x_i = E[Y \mid X = x_i]\]

For each value of \(x\) (price), the line gives the average \(y\) (sales) across all stores with that price. That said, if the relationship is genuinely nonlinear, then the linear OLS line is only an approximation of the true conditional mean even within the population.

In the case with one regressor (independent variable), the two coefficients \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) completely determine the OLS line. In terms of their interpretation we typically focus on the slope (\(\beta_1\)): how much does the conditional mean of Sales change when the Price increases by one unit? The intercept (\(\beta_0\)) is mainly a technical parameter anchoring the line without a meaningful interpretation.

Note📚 Background: Why OLS? Three Ways to Draw a Line

There are many ways to draw a line through a scatter plot. The Rmd document Stoetzer (2020) illustrates three:

  1. OLS (vertical distances): minimise \(\sum e_i^2\) where \(e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\). This is the standard regression of Y on X.
  2. Reverse regression (horizontal distances): minimise horizontal distances — this gives the regression of X on Y, which is a different line.
  3. Orthogonal regression (perpendicular distances): minimise the shortest distance from each point to the line — used in Principal Component Analysis.

OLS (vertical distances) is appropriate when \(x\) is measured without error and we want to predict \(y\). The choice of distance measure is a modelling decision, not a technical one.

Key property of OLS residuals (follows from the first-order conditions):

\[\sum_{i=1}^n e_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{e} = 0\]

The residuals always sum to zero for any OLS regression with an intercept. This means: the OLS line always passes through the point \((\bar{x}, \bar{y})\).

3.4 The OLS Formula and Its Link to Correlation

OLS selects \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) to minimise the sum of squared residuals:

\[\min_{\beta_0,\, \beta_1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)^2\]

The analytical solutions (from setting first-order conditions to zero) are:

\[\beta_1 = \frac{\text{Cov}(x, y)}{\text{Var}(x)} = r_{xy} \cdot \frac{s_y}{s_x} \qquad \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \cdot \bar{x}\]

The slope \(\beta_1\) is directly proportional to the correlation \(r_{xy}\) — scaled by the ratio of standard deviations to express it in the original units. This means the sign of the slope always equals the sign of the correlation.

3.5 The Coefficient of Determination R²

The total variation in \(y\) decomposes into explained and unexplained parts:

\[\underbrace{\sum_i(y_i - \bar{y})^2}_{\text{TSS}} = \underbrace{\sum_i(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2}_{\text{ESS}} + \underbrace{\sum_i e_i^2}_{\text{SSR}}\]

\[R^2 = \frac{\text{ESS}}{\text{TSS}} = 1 - \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{TSS}} \in [0, 1]\]

Noteℹ️ Interpreting R² Correctly

\(R^2 = 0.15\) means: 15% of the variance in Sales is explained by Price alone. The remaining 85% is attributable to other factors not yet in the model.

A low R² is not a failure — it is diagnostic information. It tells us precisely that important variables are missing. In our case, it motivates adding ShelveLoc and Advertising in the Demo.

For simple regression only: \(R^2 = r_{xy}^2\) — the square of the Pearson correlation.

3.6 What the Regression Table Shows

When we fit an OLS model on our complete population of 400 stores, the output from lm() includes more columns than we need today:

Column Meaning Today
estimate Population parameter \(\beta_0\) or \(\beta_1\) ✅ Focus here
std_error Standard error (sampling concept) → PVA2
statistic t-value (sampling concept) → PVA2
p_value p-value (sampling concept) → PVA2
lower_ci / upper_ci Confidence interval (sampling concept) → PVA2
Importantℹ️ Parameters, Not Estimates

Since we observe all 400 stores, \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) are population parameters — calculated exactly from complete data, not estimated from a sample. There is nothing to estimate: the OLS formula applied to the full population produces the exact coefficients, not approximations.

The column labelled estimate in the regression output is therefore a slight misnomer in this context. We focus exclusively on it today. Standard errors, t-values, p-values, and confidence intervals are sampling concepts — they quantify how results would vary across hypothetical repeated samples from a larger population. These columns become meaningful in PVA2.

Note📚 Background: Parameter, Statistic, and Estimator

Three concepts that students often conflate — here is the precise distinction:

Parameter: a numerical characteristic that describes the population as a whole. It may be observable (when we have complete population data, as in this Lab) or unobservable (when we only have a sample). In Lab 3: \(\beta_1 = -0.053\) is the exact slope for all 400 stores — a known population parameter.

In Session 3 and beyond, we will work with unobservable population parameters \(\beta_0, \beta_1\) that must be inferred from a sample.

Statistic (sample statistic): a numerical characteristic of a sample. Unlike a parameter, a statistic varies from sample to sample. If we drew a random sample of 100 stores from our 400, the OLS slope computed from that sample would be a statistic \(\hat\beta_1\) — and a different sample of 100 would give a different value. This sampling variability is the foundation of inferential statistics.

Estimator: a rule or formula for computing a statistic that approximates an unknown parameter. OLS is an estimator in inferential statistics: \[\hat\beta_1 = \frac{\text{Cov}(x, y)}{\text{Var}(x)}\] Applied to sample data, this formula produces an estimate of the unknown \(\beta_1\). Properties like unbiasedness, consistency, and efficiency describe how well the estimator performs across repeated samples — topics for Session 3.

In Lab 3, we use OLS as a computational tool on population data. In Session 3, we will use OLS as an estimator for unknown population parameters.

Note📚 Background: moderndive — A Tidy-Compatible Wrapper for lm()

The base R function lm() produces an object of class lm, which does not behave like a tibble and can cause issues in pipes. The moderndive package ((ismay_statistical_2023?)) provides tidy wrappers:

Function What it returns
get_regression_table() Coefficients as a tibble (like tidy() from broom)
get_regression_points() Fitted values and residuals for each observation
get_regression_summaries() R², RMSE, and other model-level statistics

These functions work seamlessly in pipes and produce tibbles that can be passed directly to gt() for formatting.

Note: moderndive applies a degrees-of-freedom correction internally, consistent with the descriptive (population) interpretation of regression used in this lab. The coefficients are identical to base R lm() — only the output format differs.

4 Step 1: Fit the Baseline Model

Before running lm(), write down what we are estimating:

\[\widehat{\text{Sales}}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Price}_i\]

We expect \(\beta_1 < 0\): stores with higher prices should, on average, sell fewer units. The EDA in Lab 2 confirmed this with \(r \approx -0.44\).

# Fit the simple linear regression model
mod_baseline <- tbl_carseats %>%
  lm(Sales ~ Price, data = .)          # formula: Sales as function of Price

# Regression table: tidy output via moderndive
tb_baseline <- mod_baseline %>%
                    get_regression_table() %>%
                    gt()
Show code 
tb_baseline %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(estimate, std_error, statistic, p_value, lower_ci, upper_ci),
    decimals = 4
  ) %>%
  # --- highlight slope row ---
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#fff8e1"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = term == "Price")
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = "14px") %>%
  tab_footnote("Yellow = slope for Price | Columns 2–6 are sampling concepts → PVA2")
term estimate std_error statistic p_value lower_ci upper_ci
intercept 13.6420 0.6330 21.5580 0.0000 12.3980 14.8860
Price −0.0530 0.0050 −9.9120 0.0000 −0.0640 −0.0430
Yellow = slope for Price | Columns 2–6 are sampling concepts → PVA2 
# Model quality: R², RMSE
tb_fit <- mod_baseline %>%
              get_regression_summaries() %>%
              select(r_squared, adj_r_squared, rmse, nobs) %>%
              gt()
Show code 
tb_fit %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(r_squared, adj_r_squared, rmse), decimals = 4) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = "14px") %>%
  tab_header("Baseline model quality: Sales ~ Price")
Baseline model quality: Sales ~ Price
r_squared adj_r_squared rmse nobs
0.1980 0.1960 2.5260 400
Important⚠️ A1: Interpret the Coefficients

Write out the regression equation with the actual numbers and interpret each part:

\[\widehat{\text{Sales}} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Price}\]

  1. Intercept \(\beta_0\): What does this value mean literally? Is it sensibly interpretable for a car-seat store (Price = 0)?

  2. Slope \(\beta_1\): If a store raises its price by 10 USD, what is the predicted change in Sales? (Express in units.)

  3. R²: What percentage of the variance in Sales does Price explain? Is this a “good” or “bad” model? Or is that the wrong question?

  4. RMSE: The Root Mean Squared Error is measured in the same units as Sales. What does it tell us about the average prediction accuracy?

5 Step 2: Visualise the Regression Line

Having fitted the model and read the coefficients in Step 1, we now ask: what does \(\beta_1 = -0.053\) actually look like in the data? The scatter plot makes the conditional mean interpretation concrete.

tbl_carseats %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Price, y = Sales)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, color = "#7d0a52", size = 1.8) +    # raw observations
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#D50006",                 # OLS line = conditional mean
              se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2)                      # se=FALSE: population, not sample
Show code: labels, annotation & theme 
# Extract coefficients via tidy pipe (no $ operator)
b0 <- mod_baseline %>%
  get_regression_table() %>%
  filter(term == "intercept") %>%
  pull(estimate) %>%
  round(2)

b1 <- mod_baseline %>%
  get_regression_table() %>%
  filter(term == "Price") %>%
  pull(estimate) %>%
  round(2)

r2 <- mod_baseline %>%
  get_regression_summaries() %>%
  pull(r_squared) %>%
  round(3)

tbl_carseats %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Price, y = Sales)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, color = "#7d0a52", size = 1.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#D50006",
              se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  annotate("text",                                              # equation annotation
    x = 70, y = 16,
    label = paste0("Sales\u0302 = ", b0, " + (", b1, ") \u00d7 Price\nR\u00b2 = ", r2),
    color = "#D50006", size = 3.5, hjust = 0, fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  # --- labels ---
  labs(
    title    = "Baseline Model: Sales ~ Price",
    subtitle = "OLS line = conditional mean E[Sales | Price]  |  se = FALSE: population analysis",
    x = "Price (USD)", y = "Sales (thousands of units)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
Tip💡 Why se = FALSE?

geom_smooth() draws a grey confidence band by default (se = TRUE). This band represents inferential uncertainty — how much the slope might vary across hypothetical repeated samples. Since we treat the 400 stores as the complete population, there is no sampling uncertainty. Setting se = FALSE removes a visually prominent but conceptually misleading element.

Important⚠️ A2: Read the Regression Line

  1. Pick one point in the scatter plot that lies clearly above the OLS line. What does its position mean in business terms?

  2. Pick one point that lies clearly below the line. What does its position mean?

  3. The OLS line always passes through the point \((\bar{x}, \bar{y})\). Verify this: what are the mean values of Price and Sales in the dataset?

tbl_carseats %>%
  summarise(
    mean_price = mean(Price),
    mean_sales = mean(Sales)
  )

6 Step 3: Residual Analysis

The scatter plot in Step 2 shows the OLS line, but a line can look reasonable even when the model is misspecified — as Anscombe’s Quartet demonstrated in the icebreaker. The residuals reveal what the line alone cannot show.

6.1 What Are Residuals?

\[e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = \text{Sales}_i - \widehat{\text{Sales}}_i\]

The residual measures how far a store’s actual sales deviate from the conditional mean at its price level. It is the deviation from the market benchmark:

  • \(e_i < 0\): store sells less than the price benchmark predicts → underperformer
  • \(e_i > 0\): store sells more than expected → outperformer
  • \(e_i \approx 0\): store is at the market average

This deviation score is the foundation for performance mining in →Lab 4.

6.2 Residual Table

# Compute fitted values and residuals for all 400 stores
reg_points <- mod_baseline %>%
                get_regression_points()%>% # returns: ID, Sales, Price, Sales_hat, residual
                head(10) %>% # Show first 10 rows
                gt()
Show code 
reg_points %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(Sales, Price, Sales_hat, residual), decimals = 3) %>%
  # --- colour-code extreme residuals ---
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#fff0f0"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = residual < -2)   # underperformers
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#f0fff0"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = residual > 2)    # outperformers
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = "13px") %>%
  tab_footnote("Red = residual < -2 (underperformer) | Green = residual > 2 (outperformer)")
ID Sales Price Sales_hat residual
1 9.500 120.000 7.273 2.227
2 11.220 83.000 9.237 1.983
3 10.060 80.000 9.396 0.664
4 7.400 97.000 8.494 −1.094
5 4.150 128.000 6.849 −2.699
6 10.810 72.000 9.821 0.989
7 6.630 108.000 7.910 −1.280
8 11.850 120.000 7.273 4.577
9 6.540 124.000 7.061 −0.521
10 4.690 124.000 7.061 −2.371
Red = residual < -2 (underperformer) | Green = residual > 2 (outperformer)

6.3 Residual Plot

# Augment: add fitted values and residuals to original data
pts <- mod_baseline %>%
  augment(data = tbl_carseats)   # adds .fitted, .resid, etc.

# Residuals vs. fitted values
p <- pts %>%
      ggplot(aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +
      geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "#7d0a52", size = 1.5) +    # one point per store
      geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "#D50006",                # zero reference line
                 linewidth = 1.1, linetype = "dashed") +
      geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "#502479",             # LOESS: detect patterns
                  se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8)
Show code: labels & theme 
p  +
  # --- labels ---
  labs(
    title    = "Residuals vs. Fitted Values",
    subtitle = "Ideal: random scatter around 0 | LOESS (purple) detects systematic patterns",
    x = "Predicted Sales (Ŝales)", y = "Residual"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
Tip💡 What to Look For in a Residual Plot

An ideal residual plot shows random scatter around the zero line — no systematic upward or downward trends, no U-shape, no funnel shape.

The LOESS curve (purple) helps detect patterns the eye might miss. If it deviates substantially from zero, it signals a problem with the model.

Pattern Signal
Random scatter Model captures the relationship well
U-shape (LOESS curves up then down) Non-linearity: need a curved term
Funnel shape (wider at right) Heteroskedasticity: variance grows with fitted values
Horizontal bands by colour Missing categorical variable (→ Step 4)
Important⚠️ A3: Interpret the Residual Plot

  1. Does the residual plot show random scatter, or do you detect a systematic pattern? Describe what you see.

  2. What would an ideal residual plot look like for a well-specified model?

  3. The LOESS curve (purple) is approximately flat around zero. Does this suggest a linearity problem?

7 Step 4: OVB Diagnosis

The residual plot in Step 3 looks approximately random when viewed without group information. But we established in Lab 2 that ShelveLoc is strongly associated with Sales. What happens when we bring that knowledge back into the residual plot?

p <- pts %>%
        ggplot(aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid, color = ShelveLoc)) +
        geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +  # colour = shelf location
        geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "#686868",
                   linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
        scale_color_manual(values = c("Bad"    = "#D50006",
                                      "Medium" = "#b3b2b2",
                                      "Good"   = "#502479"))
Show code: labels & theme 
p + 
  # --- labels ---
  labs(
    title    = "Residuals coloured by shelf location",
    subtitle = "Systematic colour banding = OVB fingerprint: ShelveLoc is missing from the model",
    x = "Predicted Sales (Ŝales)", y = "Residual",
    color    = "ShelveLoc"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

The systematic colour banding is the OVB fingerprint: Good (purple) clusters above zero, Bad (red) clusters below. A variable that the model should include is creating a pattern in what should be random noise.

Note📚 Background: Omitted Variable Bias (OVB)

OVB occurs when a variable that is correlated with both the regressor (\(x\)) and the outcome (\(y\)) is left out of the model. The slope coefficient \(\beta_1\) then absorbs part of the omitted variable’s effect, making it biased.

In our case, the OVB formula for the Price coefficient is:

\[\beta_{\text{Price, biased}} = \beta_{\text{Price, true}} + \underbrace{\beta_{\text{ShelveLoc}} \cdot \frac{\text{Cov(Price, ShelveLoc)}}{\text{Var(Price)}}}_{\text{omitted variable bias}}\]

From Lab 2 we know: - Good shelf locations are associated with higher prices (Cov > 0) - Good shelf locations are associated with higher sales (\(\beta_{\text{ShelveLoc}} > 0\))

Therefore: the bias term is positive, meaning \(\beta_{\text{Price, biased}}\) is less negative than the true price effect. The baseline model underestimates how strongly price reduces sales.

Important⚠️ A4: Diagnose the OVB

  1. Describe the colour pattern in the residual plot. Where do Bad (red), Medium (grey), and Good (purple) stores tend to cluster?

  2. Verify the mechanism from Lab 2: do stores with better shelf locations also charge higher prices on average?

tbl_carseats %>%
  group_by(ShelveLoc) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_price = round(mean(Price), 2),
    mean_sales = round(mean(Sales), 2),
    n          = n()
  )
  1. Now check empirically how much the Price coefficient changes when ShelveLoc is added to the model:
# Baseline: without ShelveLoc
mod_without <- tbl_carseats %>% lm(Sales ~ Price, data = .)

# Extended: with ShelveLoc
mod_with <- tbl_carseats %>% lm(Sales ~ Price + ShelveLoc, data = .)

# Compare price coefficients
mod_without %>%
  get_regression_table() %>%
  filter(term == "Price") %>%
  pull(estimate)

mod_with %>%
  get_regression_table() %>%
  filter(term == "Price") %>%
  pull(estimate)

In which direction does the Price coefficient change? Is this consistent with the OVB formula in the background box?

  1. R² check: compute the R² of mod_with. How much does it improve over the baseline? Does this match what the residual plot suggested?

8 Summary

Criterion Baseline (Sales ~ Price) Expected with ShelveLoc
R² 0.198 ~0.50 (substantially better)
β_Price -0.053 per USD Changes when OVB corrected
Residual plot Random scatter overall... Pattern disappears
...but Systematic colour banding by ShelveLoc No residual banding
Conclusion OVB: ShelveLoc missing from model Multiple regression → Demo
ImportantKey Messages of This Lab

  1. Regression = conditional mean — not a causal claim, but a benchmark. The OLS line gives the average Sales for each Price level.

  2. Population framing: the 400 stores are our complete dataset. \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) are population parameters, not estimates. Standard errors, p-values, and confidence intervals are sampling concepts — they become relevant in PVA2.

  3. Low R² = diagnostic information, not failure. R² = 0.198 tells us that Price alone explains only 19.8% of sales variance. The missing 85% is a signal: important variables are absent.

  4. The residual plot diagnoses OVB. Systematic colour banding by ShelveLoc reveals that the Price coefficient is biased by a missing variable.

  5. OVB direction: Good shelves command higher prices and generate higher sales. This positive correlation pulls \(\beta_{\text{Price, biased}}\) towards zero — the baseline underestimates how strongly price reduces sales.

NoteTransition to the Demo

In the live demo, the instructor adds ShelveLoc and Advertising to the model. You will see:

  • R² rise from ~0.198 to ~0.50
  • The Price coefficient become more negative (OVB correction)
  • The residual plot lose its systematic colour banding

This is the multiple regression model — and it leads directly to →Lab 4: Performance Mining, where residuals become store-level performance scores.

9 Bonus Tasks

Tip B1: Compute R² Manually

Verify R² from first principles using the variance decomposition TSS = ESS + SSR:

reg_points %>%
  summarise(
    TSS = sum((Sales - mean(Sales))^2),   # total variation in Sales
    SSR = sum(residual^2),                 # unexplained variation
    ESS = TSS - SSR,                       # explained variation
    R2  = ESS / TSS                        # share explained
  )

Does this match get_regression_summaries()?

Tip B2: R² = r² in Simple Regression

Show empirically that for simple (not multiple) regression: \[R^2 = r_{x,y}^2\]

r_xy <- tbl_carseats %>%
  summarise(r = cor(Price, Sales)) %>%
  pull(r)

r_xy^2

mod_baseline %>%
  get_regression_summaries() %>%
  pull(r_squared)

Why does this equality hold only for simple regression? (Hint: consider how \(\hat{y}_i\) relates to \(x_i\) in the simple case.)

Tip B3: Visualise the Variance Decomposition

For the first 20 stores, draw the three components of variance:

first20 <- reg_points %>%
  slice(1:20) %>%
  mutate(
    ybar   = mean(tbl_carseats$Sales),    # overall mean
    x_rank = row_number()
  )

ggplot(first20, aes(x = x_rank)) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = x_rank, y = ybar,      yend = Sales),
               color = "#b3b2b2", linewidth = 1) +      # TSS (grey)
  geom_segment(aes(xend = x_rank, y = Sales_hat, yend = Sales),
               color = "#D50006", linewidth = 1.2) +     # SSR (red)
  geom_segment(aes(xend = x_rank, y = ybar,      yend = Sales_hat),
               color = "#502479", linewidth = 1.2) +     # ESS (purple)
  geom_point(aes(y = Sales),     color = "#7d0a52", size = 2) +
  geom_point(aes(y = Sales_hat), color = "#D50006",  shape = 17, size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mean(tbl_carseats$Sales),
             linetype = "dashed", color = "#686868") +
  labs(title    = "Variance decomposition: TSS = ESS + SSR",
       subtitle = "Grey = TSS | Purple = ESS (explained) | Red = SSR (residual)",
       x = "Observation (rank)", y = "Sales") +
  theme_minimal()
Tip B4: First Performance Ranking

Using the baseline residuals, identify the 10 most extreme underperformers:

reg_points %>%
  arrange(residual) %>%
  head(10) %>%
  select(ID, Sales, Price, Sales_hat, residual) %>%
  left_join(
    tbl_carseats %>% mutate(ID = row_number()) %>%
      select(ID, ShelveLoc, Advertising, Urban, US),
    by = "ID"
  ) %>%
  gt() %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(Sales, Sales_hat, residual), decimals = 2) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#fff0f0"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = residual < -3)
  )

What do the top underperformers have in common? Look at their ShelveLoc category — is this consistent with the OVB diagnosis?

References

Stoetzer, Matthias-W. 2020. Regressionsanalyse in der empirischen Wirtschafts- und Sozialforschung Band 2: Komplexe Verfahren. Berlin, Heidelberg: Springer Berlin Heidelberg.
Source Code
<!-- ============================================================
INSTRUCTOR CUSTOMISATION — update before rendering for each cohort

  Search for "Lab 2" to verify back-references are accurate
  Search for "Demo" to verify forward-reference to multiple regression demo
  Search for "PVA2" to verify session references are correct
  The moderndive package: verify it is still on CRAN before semester start

============================================================ -->
---
title: "Lab 3: Baseline Model --- Simple Linear Regression"
subtitle: "CRISP-DM Phases 3 & 4 --- Data Preparation & Modelling"
author: "Prof. Dr. Jörg Schoder"
date: today
lang: en-US
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    theme: cosmo
    css: ../../css/styles.css
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: true
  message: false
  warning: false
bibliography: ../../lit/my_bib.bib
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false
library(tidyverse)    # data wrangling & ggplot2
library(moderndive)   # get_regression_table(), get_regression_points()
library(broom)        # augment() for residual diagnostics
library(gt)           # formatted tables
library(patchwork)    # combine ggplots
library(ISLR2)        # Carseats dataset

tbl_carseats <- tibble(Carseats) %>%
  mutate(ShelveLoc = factor(ShelveLoc,        # correct level order from Lab 2
                            levels = c("Bad", "Medium", "Good")))
```

## Learning Objectives and Overview

:::{.callout-note}
## Learning Objectives

- Understand simple linear regression as a **descriptive tool**: conditional means,
  not causal claims
- Read and interpret the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression equation and its coefficients correctly
- Compute and interpret the **coefficient of determination R²** ---
  and understand why a low R² is information, not failure
- Use **residuals** as a measure of store-level underperformance
- Diagnose **Omitted Variable Bias (OVB)** visually in the residual plot
:::

**Scenario:** In Lab 2 you explored the Carseats data and established that
`Price` is negatively correlated with `Sales` ($r \approx -0.44$).
Now you build the simplest possible regression model --- the **baseline** ---
and critically assess what it reveals and what it hides.

## 🧭 How to Navigate This Lab

| Symbol | Box colour | Meaning | When to read |
|--------|-----------|---------|-------------|
| ⚠️ | <span style="color:#e02424">**Red — Action Required**</span> | Run code, interpret output, answer questions | **Do this in the live session** |
| 💡 | <span style="color:#2f9e44">**Green — Best Practice / Tip**</span> | Coding tips, R functions, shortcuts | Skim in the lab |
| 📚 | <span style="color:#1c7ed6">**Blue, collapsed — Background Theory**</span> | Concepts, theory, derivations | Read at home |
| ℹ️ | <span style="color:#1c7ed6">**Blue, open — Note**</span> | Short clarifications | Read when reached |

⏱ **40 minutes** for mandatory tasks (A1--A4). Bonus tasks if time permits.

---

## Icebreaker: The Same Line --- Different Worlds

In Lab 2 the Datasaurus Dozen showed that identical summary statistics can hide
completely different data structures. Regression is no different: four datasets can yield the same fitted line — identical slope, intercept, and $R^2$. Fitting a line and reading its equation is not enough.

```{r anscombe-stats}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code: Anscombe summary statistics"

# Anscombe's Quartet: four datasets with (almost) identical regression statistics
anscombe_tidy <- anscombe %>%
  mutate(observation = seq_len(n())) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(-observation,
                      names_to = c(".value", "set"),
                      names_pattern = "(.)(.)")  %>%
  mutate(set = c("I", "II", "III", "IV")[as.integer(set)])

# Summary: same correlation, same slope, same R² across all four
anscombe_tidy %>%
  group_by(set) %>%
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    mean_x   = round(mean(x), 2),
    mean_y   = round(mean(y), 2),
    cor_xy   = round(cor(x, y), 3),       # Pearson correlation
    slope    = round(coef(lm(y ~ x))[2], 3),  # OLS slope
    r_sq     = round(cor(x, y)^2, 3)      # R² = r² (only for simple regression)
  ) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "Anscombe's Quartet --- summary statistics",
    subtitle = "All four datasets have nearly identical regression statistics"
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = "13px")
```

Now look at the diagram showing the data and the fitted OLS line:

```{r anscombe-plots}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code: Anscombe scatter plots"
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 9

anscombe_tidy %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(color = "#7d0a52", size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8) +   # raw observations
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE,                      # OLS line
              color = "#D50006", linewidth = 1.2) +
  facet_wrap(~set, ncol = 4) +                                # one panel per dataset
  # --- labels ---
  labs(
    title    = "Anscombe's Quartet --- same line, different worlds",
    subtitle = "Panel I: linear ✓ | II: curved | III: outlier in y | IV: single high-leverage outlier",
    x = "x", y = "y"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))
```

:::{.callout-important}

## The Central Lesson for Regression

Panel I shows a genuinely linear relationship: here the OLS line is appropriate.
Panels II--IV show cases where the same line misleads: curvature, a single outlier,
and a single high-leverage outlier each produce the same fitted line but completely different underlying structures.

**Consequence:** Fitting a regression model is not enough. The residuals must always
be inspected visually. This is what Step 3 of this lab teaches.

In **this lab** (as in the whole session 1) we treat our data as being the complete population (**descriptive regression**). Then the predicted values $\hat{y}_i$ on the OLS line are interpreted as the **conditional means**: what a store *on average* achieves *at a given* price. The residual $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ measures the deviation from this benchmark. Note that the terminology of the `r fontawesome::fa('r-project')`-output in the regression table is potentially misleading since the column "estimate" has an inferential connotation.
:::

---

### Background: Where We Are in CRISP-DM

In Lab 2 (Phase 2: Data Understanding) we established the key EDA findings:
`Price` is negatively correlated with `Sales`, `ShelveLoc` creates large group
differences, and the log transformation makes `Sales` distribution worse (more skewed), not better.
Now we enter Phases 3 & 4: we prepare the data and fit the first model.

```{r crisp-overview}
#| echo: false

tibble::tribble(
  ~Phase, ~Name,                   ~Content,                                           ~Status,
  "1",    "Business Understanding","Goal: identify underperforming stores",            "Done (lecture)",
  "2",    "Data Understanding",    "EDA: distributions, correlations, OVB signals",   "Done (Lab 2)",
  "3",    "Data Preparation",      "Factor recoding, zero handling, variable selection","Done (Lab 2)",
  "4",    "Modelling",             "Baseline model: Sales ~ Price",                   "← This lab",
  "5",    "Evaluation",            "R², residuals, OVB diagnosis",                    "← This lab",
  "6",    "Deployment",            "Performance ranking, managerial memo",             "Lab 4"
) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#f0fff0"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = Phase %in% c( "4", "5"))
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#f5f0fa"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = "13px")
```

---

### Background: Simple Linear Regression as a Descriptive Tool

#### What Regression Is --- and What It Is Not

The term regression analysis is typically associated with inferential statistics: estimating parameters from a sample and making claims about a larger population. But regression is a broader concept, that can also be used as a descriptive technique for summarising relationships in data. **This lab** covers **only the descriptive** case. That means we treat our data on the 400 stores as complete **population**. Hence we do not need to generalize from a sample to a larger world. We are describing what is already in front of us.

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: The Etymology and History of Regression

The term *regression* derives from the Latin *regredi* (to go back, to return).
It was introduced into statistics by **Francis Galton (1822--1911)**, who observed
that the children of unusually tall parents tend to be taller than average ---
but not as tall as their parents. Extreme characteristics *regress* towards the mean
across generations.

The modern statistical meaning is broader: regression is any method that describes
how the conditional mean of one variable changes with another. The "returning"
interpretation is now purely etymological.
:::

When we want to summarize how the average sales change with price across the 400 stores, the simplest way to do this is to fit a straight line through the scatter plot of `Sales` (dependent variable) vs. `Price` (independent variable). **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** fits this line by finding the line that minimizes the sum of squared vertical distances from the data points to the line. In this sense the OLS line is the best description of how average sales vary with price *across the 400 stores we have*.


:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: Exact vs. Statistical Relationships

In some situations, the relationship between two variables is exact and deterministic.
At a petrol station, the bill is exactly proportional to the number of litres filled:
$y = p \cdot x$, with no scatter around the line.

In most business situations, the relationship is statistical: the same price does
not always produce the same sales, because other factors --- shelf location,
advertising, local competition, demographics --- also play a role. The OLS line
captures the *systematic* part of this relationship; the residuals capture the rest.

$$y_i = \underbrace{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i}_{\text{systematic}} + \underbrace{e_i}_{\text{unsystematic}}$$

The statistical relationship is not a defect --- it is the reality of business data.
The residuals are the starting point for understanding *why* some stores deviate
from the systematic pattern (@stoetzer_regressionsanalyse_2020 [p. 47]).
:::

### Regression as Conditional Means

In descriptive statistics, the OLS line is a machine for computing **conditional means**:

$$\hat{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot x_i = E[Y \mid X = x_i]$$

For each value of $x$ (price), the line gives the average $y$ (sales) across all
stores with that price. That said, if the relationship is genuinely nonlinear, then the linear OLS line is only an approximation of the true conditional mean even within the population. 

In the case with one regressor (independent variable), the two coefficients $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ completely determine the OLS line. In terms of their interpretation we typically focus on the **slope ($\beta_1$): how much does the conditional mean of Sales change when the Price increases by one unit?** The intercept ($\beta_0$) is mainly a technical parameter anchoring the line without a meaningful interpretation. 



:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: Why OLS? Three Ways to Draw a Line

There are many ways to draw a line through a scatter plot.
The Rmd document @stoetzer_regressionsanalyse_2020 illustrates three:

1. **OLS (vertical distances):** minimise $\sum e_i^2$ where $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$.
   This is the standard regression of Y on X.
2. **Reverse regression (horizontal distances):** minimise horizontal distances ---
   this gives the regression of X on Y, which is a different line.
3. **Orthogonal regression (perpendicular distances):** minimise the shortest distance
   from each point to the line --- used in Principal Component Analysis.

OLS (vertical distances) is appropriate when $x$ is measured without error and
we want to predict $y$. The choice of distance measure is a modelling decision,
not a technical one.

**Key property of OLS residuals** (follows from the first-order conditions):

$$\sum_{i=1}^n e_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{e} = 0$$

The residuals always sum to zero for any OLS regression with an intercept.
This means: the OLS line always passes through the point $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.
:::

### The OLS Formula and Its Link to Correlation

OLS selects $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ to minimise the sum of squared residuals:

$$\min_{\beta_0,\, \beta_1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)^2$$

The analytical solutions (from setting first-order conditions to zero) are:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\text{Cov}(x, y)}{\text{Var}(x)} = r_{xy} \cdot \frac{s_y}{s_x}
\qquad
\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \cdot \bar{x}$$

The slope $\beta_1$ is directly proportional to the correlation $r_{xy}$ ---
scaled by the ratio of standard deviations to express it in the original units.
This means the sign of the slope always equals the sign of the correlation.

### The Coefficient of Determination R²

The total variation in $y$ decomposes into explained and unexplained parts:

$$\underbrace{\sum_i(y_i - \bar{y})^2}_{\text{TSS}} =
\underbrace{\sum_i(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2}_{\text{ESS}} +
\underbrace{\sum_i e_i^2}_{\text{SSR}}$$

$$R^2 = \frac{\text{ESS}}{\text{TSS}} = 1 - \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{TSS}} \in [0, 1]$$

:::{.callout-note}
## ℹ️ Interpreting R² Correctly

$R^2 = 0.15$ means: **15% of the variance in Sales** is explained by Price alone.
The remaining 85% is attributable to other factors not yet in the model.

A low R² is **not a failure** --- it is diagnostic information.
It tells us precisely that important variables are missing.
In our case, it motivates adding `ShelveLoc` and `Advertising` in the Demo.

For simple regression only: $R^2 = r_{xy}^2$ --- the square of the Pearson correlation.
:::

### What the Regression Table Shows

When we fit an OLS model on our complete population of 400 stores, the output
from `lm()` includes more columns than we need today:

| Column | Meaning | Today |
|--------|---------|-------|
| `estimate` | Population parameter $\beta_0$ or $\beta_1$ | ✅ Focus here |
| `std_error` | Standard error (sampling concept) | → PVA2 |
| `statistic` | t-value (sampling concept) | → PVA2 |
| `p_value` | p-value (sampling concept) | → PVA2 |
| `lower_ci` / `upper_ci` | Confidence interval (sampling concept) | → PVA2 |

:::{.callout-important}
## ℹ️ Parameters, Not Estimates

Since we observe all 400 stores, $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ are **population parameters**
--- calculated exactly from complete data, not estimated from a sample.
There is nothing to estimate: the OLS formula applied to the full population
produces the exact coefficients, not approximations.

The column labelled `estimate` in the regression output is therefore a slight misnomer
in this context. We focus exclusively on it today.
Standard errors, t-values, p-values, and confidence intervals are **sampling concepts**
--- they quantify how results would vary across hypothetical repeated samples from a
larger population. These columns become meaningful in PVA2.
:::

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: Parameter, Statistic, and Estimator

Three concepts that students often conflate --- here is the precise distinction:

**Parameter**: a numerical *characteristic that describes the population* as a whole. It may be *observable* (when we have complete population data,
as in this Lab) or *unobservable* (when we only have a sample). In Lab 3:
$\beta_1 = -0.053$ is the exact slope for all 400 stores --- a known population parameter.

In Session 3 and beyond, we will work with *unobservable* population parameters
$\beta_0, \beta_1$ that must be inferred from a sample.

**Statistic** (sample statistic): a numerical *characteristic of a sample*.
Unlike a parameter, a statistic varies from sample to sample. If we drew a random
sample of 100 stores from our 400, the OLS slope computed from that sample would
be a statistic $\hat\beta_1$ --- and a different sample of 100 would give a different value.
This *sampling variability* is the foundation of inferential statistics.

**Estimator**: a rule or formula for computing a statistic that approximates an
unknown parameter. OLS is an estimator in inferential statistics:
$$\hat\beta_1 = \frac{\text{Cov}(x, y)}{\text{Var}(x)}$$
Applied to sample data, this formula produces an *estimate* of the unknown $\beta_1$.
Properties like unbiasedness, consistency, and efficiency describe how well the
estimator performs across repeated samples --- topics for Session 3.

*In Lab 3, we use OLS as a computational tool on population data.
In Session 3, we will use OLS as an estimator for unknown population parameters.*
:::

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: `moderndive` --- A Tidy-Compatible Wrapper for `lm()`

The base R function `lm()` produces an object of class `lm`, which does not
behave like a tibble and can cause issues in pipes. The `moderndive` package
(@ismay_statistical_2023) provides tidy wrappers:

| Function | What it returns |
|----------|----------------|
| `get_regression_table()` | Coefficients as a tibble (like `tidy()` from `broom`) |
| `get_regression_points()` | Fitted values and residuals for each observation |
| `get_regression_summaries()` | R², RMSE, and other model-level statistics |

These functions work seamlessly in pipes and produce tibbles that can be passed
directly to `gt()` for formatting.

Note: `moderndive` applies a degrees-of-freedom correction internally, consistent
with the descriptive (population) interpretation of regression used in this lab.
The coefficients are identical to base R `lm()` --- only the output format differs.
:::

---

## Step 1: Fit the Baseline Model

Before running `lm()`, write down what we are estimating:

$$\widehat{\text{Sales}}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Price}_i$$

We expect $\beta_1 < 0$: stores with higher prices should, on average, sell
fewer units. The EDA in Lab 2 confirmed this with $r \approx -0.44$.

```{r baseline-fit}
#| code-fold: false

# Fit the simple linear regression model
mod_baseline <- tbl_carseats %>%
  lm(Sales ~ Price, data = .)          # formula: Sales as function of Price

# Regression table: tidy output via moderndive
tb_baseline <- mod_baseline %>%
                    get_regression_table() %>%
                    gt() 
```
```{r baseline-fit-formatting}
#| code-fold: true
tb_baseline %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(estimate, std_error, statistic, p_value, lower_ci, upper_ci),
    decimals = 4
  ) %>%
  # --- highlight slope row ---
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#fff8e1"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = term == "Price")
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = "14px") %>%
  tab_footnote("Yellow = slope for Price | Columns 2–6 are sampling concepts → PVA2")
```

```{r baseline-quality}
#| code-fold: false
# Model quality: R², RMSE
tb_fit <- mod_baseline %>%
              get_regression_summaries() %>%
              select(r_squared, adj_r_squared, rmse, nobs) %>%
              gt() 
```
```{r baseline-quality-formatting}
#| code-fold: true
tb_fit %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(r_squared, adj_r_squared, rmse), decimals = 4) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = "14px") %>%
  tab_header("Baseline model quality: Sales ~ Price")
```

:::{.callout-important icon=false}
## ⚠️ A1: Interpret the Coefficients

Write out the regression equation with the actual numbers and interpret each part:

$$\widehat{\text{Sales}} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Price}$$

1. **Intercept $\beta_0$:** What does this value mean literally?
   Is it sensibly interpretable for a car-seat store (Price = 0)?

2. **Slope $\beta_1$:** If a store raises its price by 10 USD,
   what is the predicted change in Sales? (Express in units.)

3. **R²:** What percentage of the variance in Sales does Price explain?
   Is this a "good" or "bad" model? Or is that the wrong question?

4. **RMSE:** The Root Mean Squared Error is measured in the same units as Sales.
   What does it tell us about the average prediction accuracy?
:::

---

## Step 2: Visualise the Regression Line

Having fitted the model and read the coefficients in Step 1, we now ask: what
does $\beta_1 = -0.053$ actually look like in the data? The scatter plot makes
the conditional mean interpretation concrete.

```{r scatter-fit-core}
#| code-fold: false
#| eval: false
#| fig-height: 4.5

tbl_carseats %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Price, y = Sales)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, color = "#7d0a52", size = 1.8) +    # raw observations
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#D50006",                 # OLS line = conditional mean
              se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2)                      # se=FALSE: population, not sample
```

```{r scatter-fit-layout}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code: labels, annotation & theme"
#| fig-height: 4.5

# Extract coefficients via tidy pipe (no $ operator)
b0 <- mod_baseline %>%
  get_regression_table() %>%
  filter(term == "intercept") %>%
  pull(estimate) %>%
  round(2)

b1 <- mod_baseline %>%
  get_regression_table() %>%
  filter(term == "Price") %>%
  pull(estimate) %>%
  round(2)

r2 <- mod_baseline %>%
  get_regression_summaries() %>%
  pull(r_squared) %>%
  round(3)

tbl_carseats %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Price, y = Sales)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, color = "#7d0a52", size = 1.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#D50006",
              se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  annotate("text",                                              # equation annotation
    x = 70, y = 16,
    label = paste0("Sales\u0302 = ", b0, " + (", b1, ") \u00d7 Price\nR\u00b2 = ", r2),
    color = "#D50006", size = 3.5, hjust = 0, fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  # --- labels ---
  labs(
    title    = "Baseline Model: Sales ~ Price",
    subtitle = "OLS line = conditional mean E[Sales | Price]  |  se = FALSE: population analysis",
    x = "Price (USD)", y = "Sales (thousands of units)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```

:::{.callout-tip}
## 💡 Why `se = FALSE`?

`geom_smooth()` draws a grey confidence band by default (`se = TRUE`).
This band represents inferential uncertainty --- how much the slope might vary
across hypothetical repeated samples. Since we treat the 400 stores as the
complete population, there is no sampling uncertainty. Setting `se = FALSE`
removes a visually prominent but conceptually misleading element.
:::

:::{.callout-important icon=false}
## ⚠️ A2: Read the Regression Line

1. Pick one point in the scatter plot that lies clearly **above** the OLS line.
   What does its position mean in business terms?

2. Pick one point that lies clearly **below** the line.
   What does its position mean?

3. The OLS line always passes through the point $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.
   Verify this: what are the mean values of Price and Sales in the dataset?

```r
tbl_carseats %>%
  summarise(
    mean_price = mean(Price),
    mean_sales = mean(Sales)
  )
```
:::

---

## Step 3: Residual Analysis

The scatter plot in Step 2 shows the OLS line, but a line can look reasonable
even when the model is misspecified — as Anscombe's Quartet demonstrated in the
icebreaker. The residuals reveal what the line alone cannot show.

### What Are Residuals?

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = \text{Sales}_i - \widehat{\text{Sales}}_i$$

The residual measures how far a store's actual sales deviate from the conditional
mean at its price level. It is the **deviation from the market benchmark**:

- $e_i < 0$: store sells **less** than the price benchmark predicts → underperformer
- $e_i > 0$: store sells **more** than expected → outperformer
- $e_i \approx 0$: store is at the market average

This deviation score is the foundation for performance mining in →Lab 4.

### Residual Table

```{r residuals}
#| code-fold: false

# Compute fitted values and residuals for all 400 stores
reg_points <- mod_baseline %>%
                get_regression_points()%>% # returns: ID, Sales, Price, Sales_hat, residual
                head(10) %>% # Show first 10 rows
                gt()   
```
```{r residuals-formatting}
#| code-fold: true
reg_points %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(Sales, Price, Sales_hat, residual), decimals = 3) %>%
  # --- colour-code extreme residuals ---
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#fff0f0"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = residual < -2)   # underperformers
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#f0fff0"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = residual > 2)    # outperformers
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = "13px") %>%
  tab_footnote("Red = residual < -2 (underperformer) | Green = residual > 2 (outperformer)")
```

### Residual Plot

```{r residual-plot-core}
#| code-fold: false
#| fig-height: 4

# Augment: add fitted values and residuals to original data
pts <- mod_baseline %>%
  augment(data = tbl_carseats)   # adds .fitted, .resid, etc.

# Residuals vs. fitted values
p <- pts %>%
      ggplot(aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +
      geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "#7d0a52", size = 1.5) +    # one point per store
      geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "#D50006",                # zero reference line
                 linewidth = 1.1, linetype = "dashed") +
      geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "#502479",             # LOESS: detect patterns
                  se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8)
```

```{r residual-plot-layout}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code: labels & theme"
#| fig-height: 4

p  +
  # --- labels ---
  labs(
    title    = "Residuals vs. Fitted Values",
    subtitle = "Ideal: random scatter around 0 | LOESS (purple) detects systematic patterns",
    x = "Predicted Sales (Ŝales)", y = "Residual"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```

:::{.callout-tip}
## 💡 What to Look For in a Residual Plot

An ideal residual plot shows **random scatter** around the zero line ---
no systematic upward or downward trends, no U-shape, no funnel shape.

The LOESS curve (purple) helps detect patterns the eye might miss.
If it deviates substantially from zero, it signals a problem with the model.

| Pattern | Signal |
|---------|--------|
| Random scatter | Model captures the relationship well |
| U-shape (LOESS curves up then down) | Non-linearity: need a curved term |
| Funnel shape (wider at right) | Heteroskedasticity: variance grows with fitted values |
| Horizontal bands by colour | Missing categorical variable (→ Step 4) |
:::

:::{.callout-important icon=false}
## ⚠️ A3: Interpret the Residual Plot

1. Does the residual plot show random scatter, or do you detect a systematic pattern?
   Describe what you see.

2. What would an *ideal* residual plot look like for a well-specified model?

3. The LOESS curve (purple) is approximately flat around zero.
   Does this suggest a linearity problem?
:::

---

## Step 4: OVB Diagnosis

The residual plot in Step 3 looks approximately random when viewed without group
information. But we established in Lab 2 that `ShelveLoc` is strongly associated
with `Sales`. What happens when we bring that knowledge back into the residual plot?

```{r ovb-diagnosis-core}
#| code-fold: false
#| fig-height: 4

p <- pts %>%
        ggplot(aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid, color = ShelveLoc)) +
        geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +  # colour = shelf location
        geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "#686868",
                   linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
        scale_color_manual(values = c("Bad"    = "#D50006",
                                      "Medium" = "#b3b2b2",
                                      "Good"   = "#502479"))
```

```{r ovb-diagnosis-layout}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code: labels & theme"
#| fig-height: 4
p + 
  # --- labels ---
  labs(
    title    = "Residuals coloured by shelf location",
    subtitle = "Systematic colour banding = OVB fingerprint: ShelveLoc is missing from the model",
    x = "Predicted Sales (Ŝales)", y = "Residual",
    color    = "ShelveLoc"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```

The systematic colour banding is the **OVB fingerprint**: Good (purple) clusters
above zero, Bad (red) clusters below. A variable that the model should include
is creating a pattern in what should be random noise.

:::{.callout-note collapse="true"}
## 📚 Background: Omitted Variable Bias (OVB)

OVB occurs when a variable that is correlated with *both* the regressor ($x$) and
the outcome ($y$) is left out of the model. The slope coefficient $\beta_1$
then absorbs part of the omitted variable's effect, making it biased.

In our case, the OVB formula for the Price coefficient is:

$$\beta_{\text{Price, biased}} = \beta_{\text{Price, true}} +
\underbrace{\beta_{\text{ShelveLoc}} \cdot \frac{\text{Cov(Price, ShelveLoc)}}{\text{Var(Price)}}}_{\text{omitted variable bias}}$$

From Lab 2 we know:
- Good shelf locations are associated with **higher** prices (Cov > 0)
- Good shelf locations are associated with **higher** sales ($\beta_{\text{ShelveLoc}} > 0$)

Therefore: the bias term is positive, meaning $\beta_{\text{Price, biased}}$ is
*less negative* than the true price effect. The baseline model underestimates
how strongly price reduces sales.
:::

:::{.callout-important icon=false}
## ⚠️ A4: Diagnose the OVB

1. Describe the colour pattern in the residual plot.
   Where do Bad (red), Medium (grey), and Good (purple) stores tend to cluster?

2. Verify the mechanism from Lab 2: do stores with better shelf locations
   also charge higher prices on average?

```r
tbl_carseats %>%
  group_by(ShelveLoc) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_price = round(mean(Price), 2),
    mean_sales = round(mean(Sales), 2),
    n          = n()
  )
```

3. Now check empirically how much the Price coefficient changes when ShelveLoc
   is added to the model:

```r
# Baseline: without ShelveLoc
mod_without <- tbl_carseats %>% lm(Sales ~ Price, data = .)

# Extended: with ShelveLoc
mod_with <- tbl_carseats %>% lm(Sales ~ Price + ShelveLoc, data = .)

# Compare price coefficients
mod_without %>%
  get_regression_table() %>%
  filter(term == "Price") %>%
  pull(estimate)

mod_with %>%
  get_regression_table() %>%
  filter(term == "Price") %>%
  pull(estimate)
```

   In which direction does the Price coefficient change?
   Is this consistent with the OVB formula in the background box?

4. **R² check:** compute the R² of `mod_with`. How much does it improve over
   the baseline? Does this match what the residual plot suggested?
:::

---

## Summary

```{r summary-table}
#| echo: false

# Pre-compute values for summary table
r2_baseline <- mod_baseline %>%
  get_regression_summaries() %>%
  pull(r_squared) %>%
  round(3)

b1_baseline <- mod_baseline %>%
  get_regression_table() %>%
  filter(term == "Price") %>%
  pull(estimate) %>%
  round(3)

tibble::tribble(
  ~Criterion,         ~`Baseline (Sales ~ Price)`,          ~`Expected with ShelveLoc`,
  "R²",               as.character(r2_baseline),             "~0.50 (substantially better)",
  "β_Price",          paste0(b1_baseline, " per USD"),       "Changes when OVB corrected",
  "Residual plot",    "Random scatter overall...",           "Pattern disappears",
  "...but",           "Systematic colour banding by ShelveLoc", "No residual banding",
  "Conclusion",       "OVB: ShelveLoc missing from model",  "Multiple regression → Demo"
) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#f5f0fa"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#fff0f0"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = Criterion == "Conclusion")
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = "13px")
```

:::{.callout-important}
## Key Messages of This Lab

1. **Regression = conditional mean** --- not a causal claim, but a benchmark.
   The OLS line gives the average Sales for each Price level.

2. **Population framing:** the 400 stores are our complete dataset.
   $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ are **population parameters**, not estimates. Standard errors, p-values,
   and confidence intervals are sampling concepts --- they become relevant in PVA2.

3. **Low R² = diagnostic information, not failure.**
   R² = `r r2_baseline` tells us that Price alone explains only
   `r round(r2_baseline * 100, 1)`% of sales variance.
   The missing 85% is a signal: important variables are absent.

4. **The residual plot diagnoses OVB.** Systematic colour banding by ShelveLoc
   reveals that the Price coefficient is biased by a missing variable.

5. **OVB direction:** Good shelves command higher prices *and* generate higher sales.
   This positive correlation pulls $\beta_{\text{Price, biased}}$ towards zero ---
   the baseline underestimates how strongly price reduces sales.
:::

:::{.callout-note}
## Transition to the Demo

In the **live demo**, the instructor adds `ShelveLoc` and `Advertising` to the model.
You will see:

- R² rise from ~`r r2_baseline` to ~0.50
- The Price coefficient become more negative (OVB correction)
- The residual plot lose its systematic colour banding

This is the **multiple regression model** --- and it leads directly to →Lab 4:
Performance Mining, where residuals become store-level performance scores.
:::

```{r}
#| echo: false

knitr::include_graphics('https://imgs.xkcd.com/comics/linear_regression.png')
```



---

## Bonus Tasks

:::{.callout-tip icon=false}
## `r fontawesome::fa("star")` B1: Compute R² Manually

Verify R² from first principles using the variance decomposition TSS = ESS + SSR:

```r
reg_points %>%
  summarise(
    TSS = sum((Sales - mean(Sales))^2),   # total variation in Sales
    SSR = sum(residual^2),                 # unexplained variation
    ESS = TSS - SSR,                       # explained variation
    R2  = ESS / TSS                        # share explained
  )
```

Does this match `get_regression_summaries()`?
:::

:::{.callout-tip icon=false}
## `r fontawesome::fa("star")` B2: R² = r² in Simple Regression

Show empirically that for simple (not multiple) regression:
$$R^2 = r_{x,y}^2$$

```r
r_xy <- tbl_carseats %>%
  summarise(r = cor(Price, Sales)) %>%
  pull(r)

r_xy^2

mod_baseline %>%
  get_regression_summaries() %>%
  pull(r_squared)
```

Why does this equality hold **only** for simple regression?
(Hint: consider how $\hat{y}_i$ relates to $x_i$ in the simple case.)
:::

:::{.callout-tip icon=false}
## `r fontawesome::fa("star")` B3: Visualise the Variance Decomposition

For the first 20 stores, draw the three components of variance:

```r
first20 <- reg_points %>%
  slice(1:20) %>%
  mutate(
    ybar   = mean(tbl_carseats$Sales),    # overall mean
    x_rank = row_number()
  )

ggplot(first20, aes(x = x_rank)) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = x_rank, y = ybar,      yend = Sales),
               color = "#b3b2b2", linewidth = 1) +      # TSS (grey)
  geom_segment(aes(xend = x_rank, y = Sales_hat, yend = Sales),
               color = "#D50006", linewidth = 1.2) +     # SSR (red)
  geom_segment(aes(xend = x_rank, y = ybar,      yend = Sales_hat),
               color = "#502479", linewidth = 1.2) +     # ESS (purple)
  geom_point(aes(y = Sales),     color = "#7d0a52", size = 2) +
  geom_point(aes(y = Sales_hat), color = "#D50006",  shape = 17, size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mean(tbl_carseats$Sales),
             linetype = "dashed", color = "#686868") +
  labs(title    = "Variance decomposition: TSS = ESS + SSR",
       subtitle = "Grey = TSS | Purple = ESS (explained) | Red = SSR (residual)",
       x = "Observation (rank)", y = "Sales") +
  theme_minimal()
```
:::

:::{.callout-tip icon=false}
## `r fontawesome::fa("star")` B4: First Performance Ranking

Using the baseline residuals, identify the 10 most extreme underperformers:

```r
reg_points %>%
  arrange(residual) %>%
  head(10) %>%
  select(ID, Sales, Price, Sales_hat, residual) %>%
  left_join(
    tbl_carseats %>% mutate(ID = row_number()) %>%
      select(ID, ShelveLoc, Advertising, Urban, US),
    by = "ID"
  ) %>%
  gt() %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(Sales, Sales_hat, residual), decimals = 2) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#fff0f0"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = residual < -3)
  )
```

What do the top underperformers have in common?
Look at their ShelveLoc category --- is this consistent with the OVB diagnosis?
:::




## References {.unnumbered}
